title: Anthropic Just Dethroned OpenAI. Here's What Happens Next.
summary: Anthropic has surpassed OpenAI in business adoption for the first time, prompting both companies to release competitive offers. The creator argues this marks a "free sample phase" where vendors use free or discounted access to acquire user data and lock-in, and recommends building flexible architectures to switch between tools when pricing normalizes.
writeup: A research article showing Anthropic edging past OpenAI in business adoption (34.4% vs 32.3% in April) triggered back-to-back competitive tweets: Sam Altman offering 2 free months of Codex to Claude Code switchers, Anthropic countering with a 50% usage increase on Claude Code through July. The creator argues both moves are rational given that adoption and proprietary usage data are worth more to these companies right now than subscription revenue. The through-line is a historical pricing-cycle argument: land grab, habit formation, competition thins, then a reset to real prices, a pattern already visible in Facebook Ads, Uber, DoorDash, Netflix, and AWS.
key_points: Anthropic surpassed OpenAI in business adoption for the first time in April 2026, reaching 34.4% versus OpenAI's 32.3%, according to the cited research article that triggered both companies' responses.

Users paying $200/month for Claude Code or Codex are getting output equivalent to a $5,000-$15,000/month full-time software engineer hire at a fraction of the real cost, because routing the same usage through the API would cost 5 to 25 times more in raw tokens than the flat subscription price.

Both companies are deliberately eating compute costs because they need two things more than revenue right now: adoption (creating dependency and switching cost) and proprietary usage data (the interaction patterns that make models measurably better).

Sam Altman previously admitted OpenAI was losing money on Pro subscriptions because usage exceeded projections, confirming that below-cost pricing is a deliberate strategy rather than a miscalculation.

The competitive pattern mirrors prior technology pricing cycles: Facebook Ads, Google AdWords, Uber, DoorDash, Netflix, and AWS all used subsidized pricing to build habits and thin competition before resetting to market rates.

The practical defense is tool-agnostic project architecture: if switching a project from Claude Code to Codex or any other tool takes under an hour, vendor dependency is low enough that price resets or service disruptions cause minimal disruption.
